Adhibhikshuvu Song from Sirivennela

# Adhibhikshuvu Song from Sirivennela

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Feb 13, 2026

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

1

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Share

One of my all time favorite songs on Shiva is Adi Bhikshuvu Vaadenedi Koredi from the movie Sirivennela directed by K.Viswanath Gaaru.   
  
A beautiful movie showing the bonding between a blind singer and a mute artist. The lyrics are written by Sitarama Sastry Gaaru, he came to be known as Sirivenella Sitarama Sastry after this, and music by maestro K.V.Mahadevan.  
  
*Adi Bhikshuvu Vaadenedi Koredi*  
*Boodidicchedi Vaadinedi Adigedi*  
*Edi koredi vaadinedi adigedi*  
  
What does one ask of the Adi Bhikshuvu  
What does one ask of the one who can offer only ash(Boodida).  
  
Shiva is called the Adi Bhikshuvu, the first ascetic. The opening lines emphasize what you can ask from one who is an ascetic. Again the ash reference here is Shiva's body being smeared with ashes.  
  
*Teepi raagaala Koyilammaku nalla rangunalamina vaadinedi koredi*  
*Karaku Garjanala meghamula meniki merupu hangu koorchina vaadinedi adigedi*  
  
What can one ask of him who has painted the sweet throated Kokila( Koyal) in black.  
What can one ask of him who has given the glitter of lightning to the harsh roar of thunder from the clouds.  
  
In a way the song here is a Ninda Stuti kind, where the devotee questions the decisions made by God himself.  
  
*Tenelolike poola baalalaku moonnaala aayuvicchina vaadinedi koredi  
Banda raallanu chiraayuvaga jeevinchamani aanaticchina vadinedi adigedi*  
  
What can one ask of him who has given a short life to the flowers that produce nectar.   
What can one ask of him who has blessed the rocks( Banda Rallu) to live forever.  
  
Here is a beautiful comparison, Moonalla Ayuvu in Telugu means a short life, Chiraayuvu means an Eternal life.  
  
The beautiful flowers have a very short life, while the rocks live forever, again questioning the decisions made by Shiva here.  
  
*Giribaalato tanaku kalyaana monarima darigeru manmadhuni masi chesinaadu vaadinEdi koredi*  
  
What does one ask of him, who burnt Manmadha to ashes( masi), when he tried to make him feel the desire for marriage with Parvati.  
Giri Bala here means Daughter of the Hills, reference to Parvati.  
  
*Vara garvamuna moodu lokaalu peedampa talapoyu dhanujulanu karuninchinaadu vaadinedi adigedi*  
  
What does one ask of him who blesses the Asuras who terrorize the 3 worlds, arrogant with the boons granted to them.  
  
Dhanuju is a Telugu word for Asura, the more common usage though is Daanavudu.  
  
Again here the lyrics sharply contrast Shiva's actions, on the one hand blessing the Asuras who terrorize the world, and on other hand burning Manmadha to ashes, when he tried to fill his heart with love.  
  
*Mukha preeti koreti uggu Sankarudu vaadinedi koredi*  
  
What does one ask of Shankara who is pleased by the beauty of the face itself.  
  
*Mukkanti Mukkopi mukkanti mukkopi tikka sankarudu*  
  
Mukkanti- One with 3 eyes  
Mukkopi- Literal meaning one whose anger is at the tip of his nose.  
Tikka Sankarudu- Tikka is basically Telugu for eccentric, someone whose actions cannot be explained. This is a colloquial term in Telugu for Shiva, to describe his actions that at times are hard to understand.  
  
This entire song is in the form of Ninda Stuti, where the devotee questions God, criticizes his actions, and is so philosophical, deep in it's tone.  
  
Thank you Sirivennela Gaaru for writing this, SPB Gaaru for way he renders it, Viswanath Gaaru for his picturization and KV Mahadevan for his beautiful music.
